title: Universality of magnetic susceptibility in the conical state of kagome ferromagnet Fe$_3$Sn$_2$
authors: Lilian Prodan, Donald M. Evans, Lukas Puntigam, 1 István Kézsmárki, Vladimir Tsurkan
abstract: We report universal behavior of the differential magnetic susceptibility (DMS) in the conical phase that mediates the spin-reorientation (SR) transition of the kagome ferromagnet Fe$_3$Sn$_2$. Within the SR temperature range, the DMS isotherms exhibit extremely narrow crossing regions, forming isosbestic points. Using an isosbestic-invariance analysis, we show that the isotherms collapse onto a single temperature-independent curve, revealing quadratic-in-temperature corrections to the susceptibility. Complementary field-dependent magnetic-force-microscopy measurements uncover evolution of spin textures from stripe-like domains at low fields to isolated bubble-like domains near the isosbestic field ($\sim 0.6$~T), a behavior not previously reported in bulk Fe$_3$Sn$_2$ within the conical state. These findings point to a universal mechanism for the emergence of complex magnetic textures near isosbestic points, driven by the competition between magnetocrystalline anisotropy, dipolar interactions, and external magnetic field.
summary: This paper identifies a universal scaling behavior in the magnetic susceptibility of the kagome ferromagnet Fe3Sn2 during its spin-reorientation transition. It demonstrates that magnetic isotherms collapse onto a single curve and links this phenomenon to the emergence of bubble-like magnetic textures. The findings highlight how the competition between anisotropy and dipolar interactions drives complex magnetic states.
main_problem: Understanding the physical origin of the isosbestic point in the differential magnetic susceptibility of the kagome ferromagnet Fe3Sn2 and the mechanism behind the emergence of complex magnetic textures near this point.
main_result: The study reveals a universal scaling behavior where susceptibility isotherms collapse onto a single temperature-independent curve with quadratic temperature corrections, alongside a transition from stripe-like to bubble-like magnetic domains near the isosbestic field.
method: The researchers used bulk magnetization measurements, differential magnetic susceptibility (DMS) analysis via an isosbestic-invariance approach, and Magnetic Force Microscopy (MFM) for real-space imaging of spin textures.
model_or_system: The system is the itinerant kagome ferromagnet Fe3Sn2, which undergoes a spin-reorientation transition between easy-plane, conical, and easy-axis magnetic states driven by the competition between magnetocrystalline anisotropy, dipolar interactions, and external fields.
key_observables: Differential magnetic susceptibility (DMS), magnetic domain patterns (stripe vs. bubble), and the isosbestic field (Hiso).
important_parameters: Magnetocrystalline anisotropy constants (K1, K2), demagnetizing coefficient (D), and the spin-reorientation temperature range (60 K to 130 K).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes a quadratic-in-temperature correction to the susceptibility and that the temperature dependence of the demagnetizing field is compensated by changes in the anisotropy field.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows susceptibility isotherms and their scaling collapse; Figure 3 displays MFM images of the evolution from stripe to bubble domains; Figure 4 shows temperature-driven domain pattern evolution.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the spin-reorientation transition in Fe3Sn2, presents the discovery of universal scaling in susceptibility isotherms, details the MFM imaging of domain evolution, and concludes with a discussion on the competition between anisotropy and dipolar interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Differential magnetic susceptibility χ′ of single- crystalline Fe3Sn2 (sample S1) as a function of magnetic field H applied along the c axis (a) and the a axis (b). Pan- els (c) and (d) show the scaled quantity χ∗ i , demonstrat- ing the collapse of the corresponding isotherms onto a single temperature-independent curve. The color code in panel (a) applies to all panels. The inset to panel (a) shows an enlarged view highlighting the deviation of the 125 K isotherm from those measured between 60 and 90 K. Hiso denotes the isos- bestic field.

FIG. 2. Relation between the demagnetizing coefficient D and the isosbestic field Hiso for the five studied samples (S1– S5). Large values of D correspond to out-of-plane field config- urations (H ∥c), whereas small values correspond to in-plane configurations (H ∥ab). The dashed line represents a linear fit to the data.

FIG. 4. Temperature-driven evolution of the domain pat- terns across the spin-reorientation transition in an applied magnetic field of 0.55 T. White circles mark a reference point defined by the surface morphology. Each image covers an area of 30 × 30 µm2. Insets at the bottom right indicate the mea- surement temperature.

FIG. 3. Magnetic-field evolution of the domain patterns measured at 80 K. The inset between the panels indicates the upward (yellow) and downward (brown) magnetization directions. White circles mark a reference point defined by the surface morphology. Each scan covers an area of 30×30 µm2. Insets at the bottom left indicate the applied magnetic field. Panel (e) was obtained by subtracting the image in (f) from that in (d), revealing isolated bubble-like domains.
